The potential for misuse is a concern, but so too is the lack of data that could be used to support legitimate public interest inquiries.
This paper discusses how best to approach these concerns while also protecting the rights of individuals whose information may be included in the dataset. We describe three approaches: (1) requiring researchers or analysts to obtain explicit consent before releasing any sensitive data; (2) requiring researchers or analysts to provide evidence of their intent to use the data responsibly; and (3) using an algorithm

generations.steered: This study applies the theory of reasoned action to understand citizens' decisions on public data use, using a large-scale survey experiment with 1500+ participants from the United States. We found that while there was a significant positive correlation between perceived benefits and support for the public release of datasets, this association was driven by a minority of users who were highly confident in their personal computing skills. The vast majority of users are risk-averse: only a small fraction would support the full release of sensitive information

method: FLORES-200 parallel sentences define a steering vector as mean_pooled(target_hidden) - mean_pooled(source_hidden) at primary_layer, normalized to unit norm. During generation, alpha * steering_vector is added to the residual stream at each active_layer via forward hooks. No language instructions appear in the content prompts.
